data.title: Key facts: Eli Lilly's orforglipron achieves 12.4% weight loss; Zepbound surpasses Wegovy; Operating margin rises to 40.9%
data.description:
• Eli Lilly's orforglipron, a daily oral GLP-1 agonist, showed 12.4% weight loss in trials. The FDA application is submitted, with a planned price of $149 per month.12
  • Eli Lilly's weight-loss drug Zepbound outperformed Novo Nordisk's Wegovy in a study, leading analysts to predict Zepbound will surpass Wegovy sales by 2025.3
  • Eli Lilly's adjusted operating margin has risen by 20.8 percentage points over the past two years, now standing at 40.9% for the trailing 12 months, highlighting enhanced profitability.4

data.title: Key facts: Class Action Filed Against Freeport-McMoRan; Shares Rise 1%
data.description:
• A class action lawsuit has been filed against Freeport-McMoRan Inc. for alleged securities fraud. Shareholders can apply for Lead Plaintiff status until January 12, 2026.1
  • Freeport-McMoRan Inc. (FCX) shares increased 1% to $51.14, up 35% in 2025, driven by a 3.3% rise amid a rally in precious metals as gold and silver hit record highs.2

data.title: US dollar retreats as prospect of Fed rate cuts overshadows growth data
data.description:
Key points:
  • US Q3 growth rises more than expected, dollar cuts losses
  • Drop in US consumer confidence in December weighs on dollar
  • Traders still watching for yen intervention

By Gertrude Chavez-Dreyfuss

The U.S. dollar weakened on Tuesday in a holiday-shortened week after data showing strong growth in the world's largest economy failed to shift sentiment on a currency under pressure from expectations of Federal Reserve interest rate cuts next year.

The report bolstered views that the Fed will hold off on cutting rates at its meeting in late January, with the odds currently at 87%, according to LSEG estimates. U.S. rate futures now expect the U.S. central bank's next policy easing will occur in June, with two quarter-percentage-point cuts priced in for 2026.

"We could see a lower dollar next year at least in the first quarter because the Fed is going to be increasingly forced to admit that the labor market is not in a good place," said Erik Bregar, director of FX and precious metals risk management at Silver Gold Bull in Toronto.

"The Fed may be forced to cave a little bit (on rate cuts), even more than they have so far. The market wants cuts. And the odds are that we're going to get a dovish Fed chair that will try to make that happen."

U.S. gross domestic product rose at a 4.3% annualized rate in the last quarter, the first estimate from the Commerce Department's Bureau of Economic Analysis showed. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast GDP would rise at a 3.3% pace in the third quarter.

The dollar trimmed its losses against the yen and euro following the release of the GDP report.

"A surface-level read on this (GDP) data gives the impression that the economy is roaring into an acceleration following a very short stumble around the anticipation of tariff announcements," Tom Simons, chief U.S. economist at Jefferies, said in a research note.

"However, it is difficult to see how there won't be substantial downward revisions in the final cut, or dramatic payback in Q4."

Following the GDP report, the dollar cut its losses against the yen and last traded at 156.26 yen , still down 0.5%.

The euro also pared gains against the dollar after the data and was last at $1.1779 , still up 0.2% on the day.

JAPAN INTERVENTION WATCH

The yen gained against the dollar in earlier trading after the strongest indication yet from authorities of Tokyo's readiness to intervene.

The Japanese currency hovered near lows against its major peers in recent sessions, with the threat of intervention keeping yen bears at bay. But near-term yen weakness is likely to persist, analysts say, as the cautious tone from the Bank of Japan last week hinted at a slow pace of rate hikes next year.

Japanese Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama said on Tuesday that Japan has a free hand in dealing with excessive moves in the yen.

"I would call it an un-hawkish rate hike last week from the Bank of Japan. It feels like every time we go into a BOJ meeting, the market gets too excited that we're going to have the start of a rate-hiking cycle, but the officials always tamp down those expectations," said Silver Gold Bull's Bregar.

"So a lot of the price action is really just people getting out of those bets on the yen going up, after they just keep getting disappointed."

Elsewhere in currency markets, the dollar index , which measures the U.S. currency against six rivals, slipped 0.2% to 98.02, extending its losses into a second straight day. The index fell to its lowest level since early October and was on course for a 1.4% decrease for the month, which would be its biggest fall since August, and a 9.6% drop for the year, which would be its steepest annual decline since 2017.

Strategists at MUFG said the dollar's drop this year is unlikely to be a one-off deal.

The dollar also dipped after data showed U.S. consumer confidence worsened in December. The Conference Board said on Tuesday its consumer confidence index fell 3.8 points to 89.1 this month. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast the index would be at 91.0.

In other currencies, sterling rose 0.2% against the dollar to $1.3483, after earlier hitting a 12-week peak of $1.35.

Against the Swiss franc, the greenback fell 0.4% to 0.7886 francs . Earlier in the session, the dollar dropped to a three-month low of 0.7867 francs.

data.title: Key facts: JPMorgan forecasts strong U.S. economy; explores crypto trading; Javice guilty in fraud case
data.description:
• JPMorgan analysts predict a strong U.S. economy, fueled by resilience and an AI supercycle, leading to record capital spending and fast earnings growth.1
  • JPMorgan Chase & Co. is in the early stages of exploring the potential to offer cryptocurrency trading services to its institutional clients, although this initiative has not yet been confirmed.2
  • Charlie Javice, found guilty of defrauding JPMorgan Chase in the $175 million purchase of her startup Frank, has JPMorgan covering her legal costs per their agreement.3

data.title: Key facts: Italy fines Apple €98.6M; Texas judge blocks age law; iPhone 17 lead times cut
data.description:
• Italy fined Apple (AAPL) €98.6 million for allegedly misusing its market power in the App Store. The company plans to appeal, citing privacy protections in its framework.1
  • A Texas judge blocked a law requiring app stores, including Apple, to verify user age for parental consent, citing potential First Amendment violations. This is seen as a win for Apple.2
  • Apple Inc. has cut lead times for the iPhone 17, with global delivery now averaging three days. In the U.S., all models average two days, with quick availability in China and Europe.3

data.title: Key facts: Goldman Sachs forecasts S&P 500 rise; GDP growth at 2.1%; boosts Japan mid-caps
data.description:
• Goldman Sachs forecasts an 11% increase for the S&P 500 in 2026, consistent with overall market expectations for earnings growth.1
  • Goldman Sachs projects 2026 GDP growth at 2.1%, slightly above the 2% consensus. Inflation is expected to be around 2.5%, aligning with the Federal Reserve's 2.3% growth estimate.2
  • Goldman Sachs plans to increase its investment in mid-cap companies in Japan.3

data.title: Key facts: FuelCell Energy secures $25M for South Korea project; targets data center market
data.description:
• FuelCell Energy Inc. obtained $25 million in financing from the Export-Import Bank for a project in South Korea, aimed at producing fuel cell modules for the Gyeonggi Green Energy site.1
  • FuelCell Energy Inc. is focusing on the data center market, aiming to enhance product efficiency to meet rising demand for reliable power solutions in energy-intensive sectors.2

data.title: Key facts: Ford Q3 revenues rise 9.4% to $50.53B; Maverick sales up 76%; 45K vehicles recalled
data.description:
• Ford's Q3 revenues hit $50.53 billion, up 9.4% year-on-year, beating forecasts. EPS and EBITDA also exceeded estimates, boosting stock price by 9% to $13.45.1
  • Ford's Maverick pickup truck sales surged 76% in November, indicating a rising consumer shift towards affordable vehicle options amid increasing prices.2
  • Ford Motor Company is recalling 45,047 vehicles in the U.S. due to a potential loss of exterior lighting, which can reduce visibility and increase the risk of a crash.3

data.title: Key facts: Evercore ISI raises Dollar General price target to $135; shares show market optimism
data.description:
• Evercore ISI Group has increased the price target for Dollar General shares from $130.00 to $135.00.1
  • Dollar General trades at over 18 times earnings, up from below 13, indicating market optimism for higher earnings next year, driven by strong second-half performance.2

data.title: Key facts: Coinbase Global rises 3.2%; expands into stock trading; transfers 5,869 BTC
data.description:
• Coinbase Global (COIN) rose 3.2% on Monday, fueled by its market expansion and new financial products, including acquiring The Clearing Company and a stake in CoinDCX.1
  • Coinbase has expanded into stock trading, prediction markets, and tokenized assets, and joined the S&P 500 Index, with shares stable since the year's start.2
  • Coinbase Global transferred 5,869 BTC worth about $513.8 million to an unknown wallet, indicating a possible inventory movement rather than a customer withdrawal.3

data.title: Key facts: CarMax finances 42.3% of used vehicle sales; Q2 earnings miss
data.description:
• CarMax Auto Finance financed 42.3% of retail used vehicle sales, managing 1 million accounts with a $16.53 billion auto loan portfolio.1
  • On September 25, 2025, CarMax reported Q2 earnings of $0.64 per share and $6.6 billion in sales, missing expectations. The stock dropped 20.07% to $45.60.2

data.title: Key facts: Broadcom, OpenAI to Develop AI Accelerators; Stock Surges
data.description:
• Broadcom partners with OpenAI to create 10 gigawatts of AI accelerators, with chip designs starting in late 2026 and continuing through 2029.1
  • Broadcom (AVGO) stock surged after UBS upgraded its price target and boosted AI revenue forecasts, fueling a broader rally in semiconductor stocks.2
  • Broadcom executives, including CEO Hock Tan, have sold shares for personal reasons. The company continues to supply data centers and AI clients, driven by strong demand for AI chips.3

data.title: Key facts: Bloom Energy secures $600M credit line; Q3 revenue rises 57%
data.description:
• Bloom Energy has secured a $600 million credit line with Wells Fargo, maturing December 19, 2030. The facility supports working capital and capital expenditures, with interest based on Term SOFR.123
  • Bloom Energy's Q3 revenue hit $519 million, up 57.1% year-on-year, beating forecasts by 22.8%. Despite strong earnings, the stock fell 18% to $92.89.4

data.title: Key facts: Blackstone's Stock Doubles; Set to Acquire Hamilton Island
data.description:
• Blackstone's stock has more than doubled in the last five years, reflecting a trend among private equity firms towards a fee-based model, contributing to significant valuations.1
  • Blackstone is set to acquire Hamilton Island, a move that could impact its asset portfolio and trading dynamics.2

data.title: Key facts: Baird cuts Meta price target; a16z boosts investment; WhatsApp faces Russian restrictions
data.description:
• Baird has lowered its price target for Meta Platforms (META) to $815.00 per share, a decrease from the prior target of $820.00.1
  • Marc Andreessen's a16z has heavily invested in Meta Platforms, boosting its status as the third-largest U.S. VC firm with $46 billion in assets amid rising fundraising trends.2
  • Meta Platforms' WhatsApp faces restrictions from Russian authorities, jeopardizing private communication for over 100 million users amid threats of a complete service block and reported outages.3

data.title: Key facts: Alphabet's Stock Rises 63%; Plans $700B Debt; Acquires Intersect Power
data.description:
• As of December 19, 2025, Alphabet, Google's parent company, saw a 63% rise in stock price this year, making it the top performer among the 'Magnificent Seven' stocks.1
  • Morgan Stanley reports that Google (Alphabet) could issue up to $700 billion in debt by 2026, backed by a strong balance sheet and high credit quality.2
  • Alphabet Inc. will buy clean energy firm Intersect Power LLC for $4.75 billion, boosting electricity access and expanding AI-related data centers.34

data.title: 'The Innocents of Florence' Review: A Complicated Humanism
data.description:
By Tara Isabella Burton

The Renaissance, Jacob Burckhardt once wrote, was a golden age for bastards. This, like so much of the popular understanding of 15th- and 16th-century Florence, is one of those truisms so often repeated as to become banal. Certainly there's some truth to the 19th-century Swiss historian's statement. Several of the Florentine Renaissance's most illustrious men were, in fact, illegitimate children, self-made not merely in the rags-to-riches sense but also in having created for themselves an identity, having had no earthly fathers to give them legitimacy or a name.

Leonardo da Vinci — whose very name indicates he's from a place, rather than of a person — was one such bastard. So, too, was the architect Leon Battista Alberti, the painter Filippino Lippi, and Giulio de' Medici (later Pope Clement VII). Their stories are the ones we hear most often about the Renaissance, their narratives linked to Burckhardt's grander point: that the Renaissance was a time of triumph for individuals who became masters of their own destiny. The Renaissance man, the father of the modern age, can claim all of us as successors.

But Renaissance Florence had plenty of the other, more common kind of bastard, too, as Joseph Luzzi tells us in his poignant, if at times meandering, "The Innocents of Florence," a history of Europe's first de facto orphanage, the Ospedale degli Innocenti. The Innocenti, which took in its first child in 1445, was technically not classified as an orfanotrofio, or orphanage, until the 19th century; until then it was called an ospedale, or hospital, despite its focus on housing and raising the parentless. Not that the children who passed through the Innocenti's chambers were necessarily parentless: The vast majority were illegitimate, the result of reckless or criminal liaisons their natural parents found it necessary to cover up.

The children of Mr. Luzzi's book are, in other words, the dark side of the Italian Renaissance. Their care was funded by great commercial wealth — among the most compelling characters here is the man whose bequest first financed the Innocenti: Francesco Datini, a preposterously wealthy, libidinous and melancholy-prone merchant. Datini was known to have abandoned, to death-by-penury, at least one of his underage servant-mistresses and the illegitimate child of that union. On his deathbed, however, he had a change of heart and left his fortune to the creation of a refuge for children like the one he sired and abandoned.

These children lived in the literal shadow of Florentine artistic genius: The building in which the Innocenti was housed is a particularly brilliant example of Romanesque architecture. But the existence of the Innocenti, and the seamlessness of the process of abandoning a child there, incentivized Florentine men to act with impunity. "There was now a place," Mr. Luzzi writes, "where the unwanted child of a slave or servant could be anonymously deposited without bringing legal or moral censure on the predatory father."

Many of the Renaissance's boldface names make an appearance here. Filippo Lippi, father of the illegitimate Filippino, painted Datini. Leon Battista Alberti wrote a book on the institution of the family — rendered bittersweet by his own illegitimate identity; Mr. Luzzi quotes Alberti liberally. Filippo Brunelleschi, the architect, designed the Innocenti building, while Andrea della Robbia, the sculptor, was responsible for depicting idealized versions of the swaddled infants the institution took in.

But Mr. Luzzi's interest and sympathy are with the unremarked-upon, often nameless thousands who lived and died within the Innocenti's walls. He pays particular attention to the underexamined situation of enslaved people — 60% of illegitimate births were to women who had arrived in Florence as slaves. Many of them had been impregnated by the men they served, complicating the narrative of pioneering humanism we so often expect from Renaissance Florence.

Drawing extensively on the hospital's own records, Mr. Luzzi, a professor of literature at Bard College and the author of "Botticelli's Secret" (2022), recounts story after story — and, where possible, cites name after name — of the babies who arrived at the hospital and promptly died or else were never heard from again.

At its best, "The Innocents of Florence" is an example of a complicated humanism: An empathetic study of the perpetual interplay between good intentions, human frailties and imperfect outcomes. An account of Domenico Ghirlandaio's painting of the "Adoration of the Magi" (1488), commissioned for the Innocenti, is interspersed with stories of the institution's limited budget for food. "It is difficult to say whether spending such a lavish sum on Ghirlandaio's painting directly affected the diet of the Innocenti's children," Mr. Luzzi writes, before concluding that they likely "might have eaten more fruit and vegetables (and less bread)" had Innocenti officials "diverted Ghirlandaio's florins into the pantry." Most compelling of all is a chapter on Vincenzio Borghini, a well-meaning 16th-century director of the Innocenti who attempts to educate the foundlings under his care and to protect the girls in particular from sexual exploitation, only to be complicit in the castration of the boys (that they might sing castrato roles in church choirs) and the sequestering of the girls.

As the history leaves the Renaissance behind, Mr. Luzzi's narrative loses focus. Disquisitions on the nature of postunification "Italianness" in the 19th century or a close reading of Carlo Collodi's "Pinocchio" as a manifesto of Italian political childhood are fascinating but feel as if the Innocenti is merely an occasion for Mr. Luzzi to write, however lyrically, on elements of Italian history and identity vaguely pertaining to childhood.

Yet if all we get from "The Innocents of Florence" is a sense of elegiacal reverence for those children who briefly called it home, Mr. Luzzi's narrative is ultimately an evocative one. Perhaps, Mr. Luzzi suggests, there is no grand narrative to history after all: only individual people, all of whom deserve to be remembered.

  • Ms. Burton is the author of the novel "Here in Avalon."

data.title: Jefferies says Goodman's Europe JV for data centres de-risks FY26-28 earnings
data.description:
** Jefferies says Australian data centre landlord Goodman Group's joint venture with Canada Pension Plan Investment Board to build data centers across Europe 'de-risks' FY26-28 earnings

** Brokerage expects development margins of about 60% across the four data centre projects in Paris, Frankfurt, and Amsterdam

** In addition to the A$14 billion ($9.37 billion) partnership, co has secured planning approval for new 88MW data centre in Sydney

** Stock is down 11.3% YTD, as of late close

($1 = 1.4941 Australian dollars)

data.title: U.S. Economy Posts Robust Growth in the Third Quarter — WSJ
data.description:
By Chao Deng and Harriet Torry

Robust spending by U.S. consumers drove greater-than-expected economic expansion in the third quarter, and the strongest growth rate in two years.

Gross domestic product, the value of all goods and services produced across the economy, rose at a seasonally and inflation-adjusted 4.3% annual rate from July through September, the Commerce Department said Tuesday.

The report was delayed nearly two months by the government shutdown, and looks back at the period before the shutdown was in effect. It does, however, offer a snapshot of an economy that has managed to keep humming along for much of the year.

Growth picked up from 3.8% in the previous quarter, and easily beat the 3.2% forecast among economists polled by The Wall Street Journal.

It was the strongest expansion since the third quarter of 2023. Rising consumer spending was partly driven by healthcare, including outpatient services, and at hospitals and nursing homes. International travel, legal services and spending on products like personal computers and software also contributed.

Artificial intelligence-related spending helped, too, though the pace of growth appeared to cool from the second quarter. Overall business investment growth slowed to 2.8% in the third quarter from 7.3% in the prior three months.

The report offered fresh evidence that consumers continue to lift the U.S. economy, even as inflation remains higher than the Federal Reserve's 2% target, hiring remains sluggish and consumer-confidence readings reflect a dour overall mood.

The Commerce Department will revise the third-quarter numbers and is expected to release its assessment of economic growth for the rest of 2025 early next year.

"We don't know if everything is as good as the third-quarter number suggests, but it is sending the message that the economy is hanging in there," said Jonathan Millar, an economist at Barclays.

The new data show the economy growing at an average annual rate of 2.5% since President Trump returned to office, even after a first-quarter contraction as companies ramped up imports to get ahead of new tariffs. The growth is on par with the 2.4% average pace recorded in 2024, during the Biden presidency.

"The TARIFFS are responsible for the GREAT USA Economic Numbers JUST ANNOUNCED...AND THEY WILL ONLY GET BETTER! Also, NO INFLATION & GREAT NATIONAL SECURITY. Pray for the U.S. Supreme Court!!! President DJT," Trump wrote on Truth Social on Tuesday morning.

In November oral arguments, Supreme Court justices expressed skepticism that Trump's tariffs are authorized by a 1977 law that gives the president the power to regulate imports. The court hasn't yet issued its ruling in the case.

Tuesday's report showed inflation picked up as the economy grew strongly last quarter. Prices excluding volatile food and energy categories rose an annualized 2.9% in the third quarter, compared with 2.6% in the second quarter.

Consumer spending sped up to an annual rate of 3.5% in the third quarter.

A measure that tracks demand from businesses and consumers — but not more volatile government, inventory and international trade data — ticked up slightly in the third quarter. That suggests underlying demand from businesses and consumers remained solid.

Economists also spotted some signs of weakness. Business spending on structures that include commercial buildings and factories contracted, for example. Residential investment was down by a 5.1% annual rate for a second consecutive quarter.

Consumer spending on durable goods, which includes things like washing machines and dishwashers, was up 1.6%, slowing from 2.3% growth in the prior quarter.

"It tells a tale that people aren't replacing their worn out stock," said Joe Brusuelas, chief economist at RSM. Meanwhile spending in services, which economists say largely stems from higher-income households, grew 3.7%, accelerating from 2.6% in the prior quarter and only 0.8% in the first.

Consumer spending has been uneven, with growing signs that lower-income Americans are struggling. The GDP report showed that inflation-adjusted disposable personal income was flat, which indicates that income is barely keeping up with inflation. The measure is after taxes and excludes capital gains, which have buoyed mostly upper-income people who can afford to invest.

Economists have been watching AI spending closely as massive data centers mushroom around the country and AI stocks boost portfolios. Barclays estimated that AI-related investment contributed about 0.4 percentage point to third quarter GDP growth, down from roughly 1 percentage point in the first half of the year.

Meanwhile, trade boosted the third-quarter headline GDP number by 1.59 percentage points, as exports jumped and imports continued to decline.

GDP reflects the total of all domestic spending plus exports. Since some domestic spending is on imported products, rather than things made in the U.S., imports are subtracted from total spending to arrive at GDP estimates. When imports contract, as they did in the third and second quarters, that results in additions to GDP.

Write to Chao Deng at chao.deng@wsj.com and Harriet Torry at harriet.torry@wsj.com

data.title: Dye & Durham Clears Competition Probe, Still Faces Major Obstacles — Market Talk
data.description: Dye & Durham may have cleared one hurdle with the Competition Bureau closing its inquiry, but the company still faces a steep climb. The stock remains halted under a cease-trade order for failure to file, and the company has now confirmed it will be further delayed in submitting its audited annual and 1Q results. Kevin Krishnaratne of Scotiabank notes the embattled legal-technology and software firm is still working to complete its audit. At the same time, Dye & Durham has launched a formal sale process as it contends with strategic and financial pressure. While the end of the regulatory investigation removes one overhang, the core issues of prolonged reporting delays, covenant risks, and uncertainty around a potential sale continue to weigh heavily on the outlook. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

data.title: WPTG has closed the deal with Spotr Group AB and will issue first tranche of shares as payment
data.description:
Stockholm, Sweden, 2025-12-23 – White Pearl Technology Group AB (WPTG), a Swedish global tech company, today announced that it has closed the transaction from October 16 with Spotr Group AB (Spotr Group) and has taken ownership of Adligo AB, APTR SL AB, Appspotr South Asia (PVT) LTD, as well as a 51 percent stake in Krobier AB. In accordance with the agreement, the Board of Directors of WPTG has, pursuant to the authorization granted by the Annual General Meeting held on 20 May 2024, resolved to issue 676,036 Series B shares as the first payment (representing 70% of the total consideration) to Spotr Group AB at a subscription price of SEK 20 per share.

As announced on 16 October, WPTG has entered into an agreement with the IT Group Spotr Group AB (Spotr Group) (Nasdaq First North: SPOTR) to acquire the Spotr Group subsidiaries Adligo AB, APTR SL AB, Appspotr South Asia (PVT) LTD and a 51 percent stake in Krobier AB. In accordance with the contract, WPTG will issue in total 965,766 shares to Spotr Group and the Board of Directors in WPTG today resolved to issue 676,036 series B shares (70% of total consideration) as first installment of the payment. The remaining 30% of the total consideration will be issued in three months, provided that WPTG has not deducted any warranty claims from the deferred consideration.

Deviation from the shareholders' preferential rights

The Board of WPTG resolved to deviate from the shareholders’ preferential rights. The reasons for the deviation are that the share issue forms an integral part of the agreed transaction dated 16 October 2025, pursuant to which the Company acquired four companies from Spotr Group AB, with the share issue being carried out by way of set-off against debt relating to the acquisition. The Board considers the share issue to be on market terms, proportionate and in the best interest of the Company and its shareholders.

Number of shares and share capital

As a result of the Share Issue the Company's share capital will increase by SEK 14,872 from SEK 636,234 to SEK 651,106 through the issuance of 676,036 new shares increasing the number of shares from 28,919,738 to 29,595,774 and entailing a dilution of approximately 2.3 percent of the total number of shares and votes in the Company (based on the number of shares and votes following the Share Issue).

For more information, please contact:

Peter Ejemyr, Vice President Investor Relations

White Pearl Technology Group

Email:ir@whitepearltech.com

Phone: +46 733 611 000

The company's Certified Adviser is Amudova AB, email:info@amudova.se.

About White Pearl Technology Group:

White Pearl Technology Group AB (WPTG) is a Swedish global technology company specialising in digital transformation solutions. With a presence in over 20 countries and a team of 850 experts, WPTG helps organisations navigate the complexities of the digital age, offering services ranging from ICT and system integration to business software and digital innovation. The company is listed on Nasdaq First North (WPTGB) in Stockholm, Sweden and on OTCQX (WPTGF) in the U.S.

Attachments

WPTG has closed the deal with Spotr Group AB and will issue first tranche of shares as payment

data.title: Dye & Durham Delays Filings for Fiscal 2025, 1Q
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Dye & Durham will need more time to file its financial statements for both fiscal year 2025 and the first quarter, and will seek to push back its annual general meeting.

The embattled Canadian legal‑technology and software company said Tuesday its auditor needs extra time to review certain past accounting practices related to how revenue was recorded under previous management.

The company said its current management believes any corrections from earlier years would be minor, but the auditor has to complete its review before providing an opinion on the annual results.

Dye & Durham said that until the reports are filed a cease‑trade order issued on Dec. 15 will stay in place. That delay means the company will also ask the court for permission to push back its shareholder meeting, which had been set for Dec. 31. The order was issued because Dye & Durham failed to file its required financial statements on time, and can't delay its shareholder meeting without court approval.

Earlier on Tuesday, Canada's Commissioner of Competition discontinued its inquiry into alleged conduct at the company, and confirmed that no further investigatory steps are contemplated at this time.

In November 2024, Dye & Durham disclosed that the competition regulator had opened an investigation into its role in the conveyancing software market.

At the time, the regulator said it was examining potential anti‑competitive behavior, including practices that might limit rival software providers from offering their products or services to legal professionals.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com
